explanation: Antimicrobial Resistance occurs when bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites evolve to survive medicines designed to kill them, making infections harder to treat.
impact: 700,000+ deaths annually worldwide are attributed to drug-resistant infections.

explanation: 'Superbugs' are microorganisms resistant to multiple antimicrobial drugs, leaving very few or no treatment options.
impact: By 2050, drug-resistant infections could cause 10 million deaths annually if no action is taken.

explanation: When bacteria are exposed to antibiotics, susceptible bacteria die but resistant ones survive and multiply. Misuse accelerates this natural process.
impact: At least 30% of antibiotic prescriptions in outpatient settings are unnecessary.

explanation: AMR is interconnected across human health, animal health, and the environment. Tackling it requires coordinated action across all three domains.
impact: 73% of antibiotics sold globally are used in animals raised for food.

explanation: India is among the countries most affected by AMR due to high antibiotic consumption, over-the-counter availability, and widespread self-medication.
impact: India has one of the highest rates of antibiotic resistance globally, with over 58,000 newborn deaths annually from resistant infections.

explanation: WHO classifies antibiotics into Access (first-line, low resistance risk), Watch (higher resistance potential), and Reserve (last resort). Countries should aim for 70%+ use from the Access group.
impact: Only 3 out of 76 countries surveyed met the 70% Access group target.

explanation: Hand hygiene, vaccination, clean water, and sanitation prevent infections, reducing the need for antibiotics in the first place.
impact: Proper hand hygiene alone can reduce respiratory infections by up to 21%.

explanation: Drug-resistant infections lead to longer hospital stays, costlier treatments, and higher mortality, straining healthcare systems globally.
impact: AMR could cost the global economy up to $100 trillion by 2050 and push 24 million people into extreme poverty.

explanation: Using antibiotics to promote growth in livestock contributes heavily to AMR. Resistant bacteria can spread to humans through the food supply chain and environment.
impact: Over 70% of medically important antibiotics in some countries are sold for use in animals.

explanation: Vaccines prevent both viral and bacterial infections. Fewer infections mean fewer antibiotics prescribed, directly lowering the chances of resistance developing.
impact: Universal pneumococcal vaccination could prevent 11.4 million days of antibiotic use in children annually.

explanation: Scientists are exploring alternatives like bacteriophages (viruses that target bacteria) and specialized antibodies, but these are not yet widely available to replace antibiotics.
impact: The clinical pipeline for new antimicrobials is currently insufficient to tackle the rising threat of AMR.

explanation: Never demand antibiotics, don't share prescriptions, practice good hygiene, and ensure your vaccinations are up to date. Every action counts.
impact: Community awareness and action can significantly curb inappropriate antibiotic use.
